serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of anchovy salad is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small plate as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and casual dining settings.

nutrient_source.title: Anchovy Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Anchovy Salad (M16), cr_id G00010954, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for anchovy salad. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable salad with oil dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly fat (44.5 g/100g) and low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g), which suppresses postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (32) × carbohydrate per serving (5.9 g × 1.5 serving factor = 8.85 g) / 100 = 2.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; fish sauce and dried anchovies are both inherently very high in sodium, and potassium (64 mg/100g) is insufficient to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request preparation with reduced fish sauce and fresh rather than dried anchovies, and limit portion to no more than 50 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) from oil, peanuts, and sesame significantly increases caloric density and may worsen insulin resistance; dietary fibre is 0 g/100g providing no glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 80–100 g, request reduced oil and peanuts to lower fat load, and pair with high-fibre foods to compensate for the absence of dietary fibre in this dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (44.5 g/100g) and high sodium (810 mg/100g) are both major cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is 26 mg/100g; anchovies provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids but the overall fat and sodium profile is unfavourable.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard preparation due to the combined high-fat and high-sodium profile; if consumed occasionally, request significantly reduced oil and fish sauce and limit portion to 50 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling; chilli and tomatoes are nightshade vegetables that may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to high sodium and nightshade content (chilli, tomatoes); if anchovies are desired for their omega-3 benefit, request a preparation without nightshades and with substantially reduced fish sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Anchovy Salad (ငါးနီတူသုပ်, also known as Nga Ni Tu Thoke) is a Southeast Asian-influenced salad dish served in Singapore, featuring dried or fresh anchovies (ikan bilis) tossed with vegetables such as tomatoes and chilli, dressed with fish sauce, lime juice, oil, peanuts, and sesame seeds. The dish delivers a bold combination of salty, sour, and umami flavours with a crunchy texture from peanuts and sesame. Per 100 g, it provides 430 kcal, 44.5 g fat, 1.32 g protein, 5.9 g carbohydrate, and 810 mg sodium. The very high fat content derives primarily from the generous use of oil, peanuts, and sesame, making this a calorie-dense dish. Sodium is extremely high due to fish sauce and dried anchovies. Dietary fibre is 0 g/100g. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (sodium far exceeds safe thresholds), cardiovascular disease (high fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (nightshade ingredients and high sodium). For Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate content is a partial positive, but the high fat load and absence of fibre are concerns. Anchovies provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. This dish is listed in the Singapore HPB Foodo database under the Vegetables / Vegetable-based dishes category (cr_id G00010954).

notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this anchovy salad preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010954) as of 2026-04-16. 2. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source record. 3. GI is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable salad with oil dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 4. The input record classifies this dish under 'Vegetables / Vegetable-based dishes'; reclassified to 'Fish and seafood / Seafood-based salads and dishes' to better reflect the anchovy-primary identity. 5. Cultural primary_cuisine was listed as 'Western' in the input; corrected to 'Singaporean / Southeast Asian' based on the dish's HPB Singapore provenance and Burmese-influenced name. 6. Protein_g (1.32) and carbohydrate_g (5.9) are low but non-zero as reported by HPB; fat_g (44.54) is very high due to oil, peanuts, and sesame dressing — all values are supported by the HPB canonical source.
